coredata.dc:title: One-pot fabrication of aluminium ion anchoring boric acid-carbon dots hybrid system and its highly selective fluorescence sensing for fluorine ion in water environment

Yang, Weijie
Zhu, Wenping
Chen, Hongyu
Chen, Yahong
Liu, Ruilan
Cui, Yali
Li, Yanxia
Wang, Baodui
coredata.dc:description: Fluorine is one of the essential trace elements for human body, which is essential for calcification process in living organisms. In the paper, a “off–on” fluorescence sensor for fluorine ion based on aluminium ion and boric acid codoped carbon dots was synthesized and characterized. According to fluorescence analysis, the water-soluble sensor exhibited highly selective and sensitive for fluorine ion. In sensing process, F- could bind to Al3+ after the addition of fluorine ion, which resulted in the decomposition of Al-BA-CDs system, then intense yellow fluorescence signal appeared. Moreover, the fluorescence sensor exhibited low detecting limit (5.0 nanomolar level) by fluorescence titration spectrum. In addition, according to evaluate the fluorescence sensing properties of F- in HepG2 cell and zebra fish, this job greatly extended the use of F- sensor from aluminium ion and boric acid codoped carbon dots in organism. The work also provided an interesting idea for fabricating water-soluble fluorescence sensor for F-.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Cell imaging
Fluorine ion
Zebrafish imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The SEM images of Al-BA-CDs (a. b. c. d) and the HRTEM images of Al-BA-CDs (e. f. g. h).

A. Uv–vis titration spectrum of Al-BA-CDs for F- in pure water solution (slit = 2 nm), b. The colors of Al-BA-CDs and Al-BA-CDs + F-, c. the fluorescence selectivity experiment histogram of Al-BA-CDs for various ions (Ex = 365 nm, Em = 570 nm, slit = 5 nm), d. the fluorescence images of Al-BA-CDs for various ions under UV light (Ex = 365 nm, slit = 5 nm), e. The fluorescence titration spectrum of Al-BA-CDs for F- in pure water solution (Ex = 365 nm, slit = 5 nm), f. The fluorescence images under UV light (Ex = 365 nm) as increasing F- concentration.

The preparation process of sensor Al-BA-CDs and its solid state fluorescence image.

head.ce:title: One-pot fabrication of aluminium ion anchoring boric acid-carbon dots hybrid system and its highly selective fluorescence sensing for fluorine ion in water environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Eningeering, Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, International Joint Research Laboratory for Biomedical Nanomaterials of Henan, Zhoukou Key Laboratory of Nanosensing and Detection, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, PR China

International Joint Research Laboratory for Biomedical Nanomaterials of Henan
Zhoukou Key Laboratory of Nanosensing and Detection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Eningeering, Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, International Joint Research Laboratory for Biomedical Nanomaterials of Henan, Zhoukou Key Laboratory of Nanosensing and Detection, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, Gansu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, Gansu, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly selective and sensitive fluorescence sensor for fluorine ion from aluminium and boric acid codoped carbon dot was synthesized.

Fluorine is one of the essential trace elements for human body, which is essential for calcification process in living organisms. In the paper, a “off–on” fluorescence sensor for fluorine ion based on aluminium ion and boric acid codoped carbon dots was synthesized and characterized. According to fluorescence analysis, the water-soluble sensor exhibited highly selective and sensitive for fluorine ion. In sensing process, F- could bind to Al3+ after the addition of fluorine ion, which resulted in the decomposition of Al-BA-CDs system, then intense yellow fluorescence signal appeared. Moreover, the fluorescence sensor exhibited low detecting limit (5.0 nanomolar level) by fluorescence titration spectrum. In addition, according to evaluate the fluorescence sensing properties of F- in HepG2 cell and zebra fish, this job greatly extended the use of F- sensor from aluminium ion and boric acid codoped carbon dots in organism. The work also provided an interesting idea for fabricating water-soluble fluorescence sensor for F-.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorine is an essential trace element in the human body, which is mainly distributed in bones and teeth [1–3]. Fluorine acts on the human body according to various ways. A small amount of fluorine in the human body has a certain impact on development and growth, bone metabolism, etc., while excessive or too little fluoride can cause some diseases, and the toxicity of fluorine also has a potential impact on human health [4–8]. As the industrialization of process continues to accelerate, more and more areas like are suffering from fluorine pollution. The human body receives fluorine through a variety of ways including water, food and air [9]. The World Health Organization (WHO) confirms that fluoride content in drinking water should not exceed 1.5 ppm level. Human diet (fish and shellfish, squid, jellyfish and other molluscs are high content) and drinking water containing fluorine are major reservoir of fluorine, so it is necessary to analysis fluoride ions in the reservoirs [10–12]. To date, ion chromatography to colorimetry [13], electrochemistry [14] and atomic absorption spectrum [15] have been utilized to detect fluorine, however, the testing technology need complex sample preparation process and large scale equipment, which seriously limit the test accuracy and test efficiency. So simple, in situ and non-destructive method was very necessary for testing fluorine in environment and organism, which can highly enhance the detection efficiency. Among the monitoring and identification methods for fluorine ion, the fluorescence sensor has attracted adequate attention owing to the high selectivity, sensitivity and low detection limits [16–24]. In addition, the fluorescence sensor can realize in situ, rapid and accurate analysis. In spite of the increasing attention, the fluorescence probes for fluorine generally has been a particularly challenging task due to the anion selectivity criteria.

In the past several years, a number of fluorescence sensors for fluorine ion has been reported [25–31]. However, due to the small atomic radius and high electronegativity of fluorine, it is easy to form hydrogen bonds with hydrogen bond donors in the environment, which brings great difficulties to the detection of fluorine ions. Some small molecule fluorine ion fluorescence sensor based on Si-O, P-O and hydrogen bond have been synthesized, which exhibit specific recognition effect [32–37]. But poor water solubility, high water sensitivity, and ion interference like OH–, H2PO4 -, NO3 – limited its application in environment and organism. So the design of probe to identify fluorine ion in pure water environment has been challenging. Besides the small fluorescence, some inorganic hybird materials like MOF and quantum dots (carbon dots, Ag2S, CdTe) [38–40] have been designed and prepared for detecting fluorine ion in pure water environment. In the quantum dots derived fluorescence sensor, carbon dots have already made much progress owing to its size effect, quantum effect and fluorescence property. But the fluorine ion fluorescence sensor from carbon dots derived system was relative lack of in-depth research [41–43]. In the paper, according to one-pot hydrothermal reaction, aluminium ion mediated boric acid anchoring carbon dots hybrid was prepared. In the hybrid system, aluminium ion can interact with some active groups like carbonyl, hydroxide and amidogen, which can quench the fluorescence carbon dots system. In addition, boric acid can anchor the carbon dots and enhance drastically the water solubility. In pure water environment, after adding fluorine ions, aluminum ion is captured and formed [AlF6]3-complex ion, which induced the strong fluorescence emission of carbon dots by “off–on” process. Simultaneously, by HepG2 cell and zebra fish fluorescence imaging, Al-BA-CDs can track fluorine ion quickly in vivo. In addition, based on the high selectivity of water-soluble fluorine ion fluorescence probe, in natural environment and organism, the “off–on” fluorescence sensor also show potential application for measuring fluorine ion in situ. All the results proved that the proposed strategy is feasible to design “off–on” fluorescence probes for analyzing fluorine ion. This work provides import theoretical and practical basis for sensitive fluorine ion fluorescence sensor.

o-phenylenediamine, boric acid and aluminium nitrate were purchased from Aladdin co.,Shanghai, China. All the chemicals containing solvents are of reagent grade and are used without further purification..

1HNMR spectra are recorded by Varian VR500-MHz spectrometer with TMS as an internal standard. The UV–Vis spectra are recorded on Perkin-Elmer Lambda-35 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence are tested on Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer. The FTIR spectrum was obtained by bruker INVENIO instrument. The SEM images was tested by F250 thermal field emission scanning electron microscopy. The HRTEM and Mapping are tested by FEI Titan G2 60–300 electron microscope. XRD was tested by D8 FOCUS instrument, HR-ESI-MS was tested by microTOF-QII10410 instrument. The XPS were obtained by thermo kalpha spectrometers. The fluorescence images of HeLa cells and zebra fish are obtained using olympus fv1300 laser confocal fluorescence microscopy. The absolute quantum yield was measured by FLS 920 steady state-transient fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing F- in pure water environment
ce:section.ce:para: All spectroscopic were measured in water solution and pH (7.4) was regulated by HEPES buffer solution. In sensing titration experiments, 2 mL aqueous solution containing 100 μL solution of Al-BA-CDs (1 mg/mL) was filled in a quartz optical cell of 1 cm optical path length. 10 μL F- stock solution (1.0 × 10-3 M) was added to above solution with a micro-pipette. The spectra signal was collected after addition F- into the solution for 10 s. The excitation wavelength of fluorescence spectrum was 365 nm.

The binding constant between sensor Al-BA-CDs and F- was calculated by the linear Benesi-Hildebrand expression [44]
I 0
/
( I -
I 0
)
=
I 0
/
[ s e n s o r ]
+
I 0
/
[ s e n s o r ]
∙
K s
∙
[ F - ]

Where I is the change in the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm. [sensor] and [F-] are the concentration of Al-BA-CDs and F-, respectively. I0 is the intensity of Al-BA-CDs in the absence of F-. On the basis of the plot of 1 / [I] versus 1 / [ F-], the stability constant Ks is confirmed.

The detection limit (q min) Al-BA-CDs for F- in pure water environment was calculated by the equation.
q
m
i
n
=
3
s
b
S

In the equation, q min was the detection limit, sb was the standard deviation of a blank signal, S was the sensitivity of fluorescence method for analyzing F- at 570 nm. According to the ratio equation, the detection limit was confirmed.
The synthesis routine of Al-BA-CDs was shown in Scheme 1 .

2 g o-phenylenediamine and 4 g boric acid were dissolved in 50 mL two-distilled water in a heated state, then 0.5 g aluminium nitrate was added to above solution, A mixed solution was obtained under stirring and heating condition. Then adding the mixed solution to a high pressure reactor, and heating the mixed solution for 4 h at 220 ℃. After natural cooling, the reaction product was filtered for three time, which can remove the reaction residues and produce a clear solution. The clear solution was continuously heated at 90 ℃ to volatilize portion water solvent. In the natural room temperature condition, earthy crystal dissolved from the rest of the solution. By vacuum drying, 4.25 g target compound Al-BA-CDs was prepared. As shown in Scheme 1, Al-BA-CDs was brown, and it exhibited no obvious fluorescence under UV light (Scheme 1). Furthermore, it needed to be characterized by other testing means.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Al-BA-CDs by IR, XRD, SEM, TEM, EDS and element mapping
ce:section.ce:para: From IR and XRD spectra, Al-BA-CDs was characterized preliminarily. Compared with the IR spectrum of o-phenylenediamine, Al-BA-CDs exhibited striking difference, which revealed the characteristic banks of o-phenylenediamine disappeared.

The band at 3180 cm−1 in Fig. S1 corresponded to the stretching vibration absorption of the O − H functional group. The bands located at 1658.99 cm−1 and 1409.41 cm−1 were ascribed to C = O and B − O bands, respectively. In addition, the sharp peak located at 1195 cm−1 was attributed to the asymmetrically stretched vibration of B − O − B, demonstrating the formation of polyboric acid in Al-BA-CDs system. The XRD (Fig. S2) of pattern Al-BA-CDs showed that a diffraction peak existed at 2θ = 20.0°, which was corresponded to the diffraction peak of diffraction pattern (002) of graphite carbon from carbon dots. The XRD result indicated that Al-BA-CDs had a graphite-like structure, which needed be confirmed by HRTEM analysis.

By scanning electron microscope (SEM), as shown in Fig. 1 . a. b. c. d, Al-BA-CDs showed uniformly granular structure. According to fine observation, the particle was not monodispersed and there is a distinct aggregation. We deduced that the aggregation of particle was from the intermolecular forces of boric acid. In addition, high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1. e. f. g. h) displayed very unique morphology appearance. And very clear crystal lattice emerged in aggregation particles. According to analysis the lattice fringe carefully, two kinds of lattice distances (0.35 nm and 0.21 nm) were captured. We found that the materials with a 0.35 nm lattice distance always surrounded the materials with a 0.21 nm lattice. We deduced that the composite and differentiated lattice characteristics was from the complex structure of aluminium, boric acid and carbon dots. Especially, the materials based on 0.21 nm lattice was inferred as carbon dots. Some active groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino are distributed on the surface of carbon dots, which could coordinate with aluminium ion. Additionally, boric acid could also participate coordination process of aluminium ion. According to the coordination reaction, the fluorescence of carbon dots was quenched strongly. The system Al-BA-CDs showed no obvious fluorescence in pure water solution.

Moreover, the EDS spectrum (Fig. S3) indicated the qualitative distribution of C, N, O, B and Al elements. The element mapping showed C, N, B, O and Al elements all appeared in the materials (Fig. S3). And the distribution of aluminum elements was relatively sparse. All the above consequences proved that Al-BA-CDs system from theoretical initial design was successfully prepared by one-pot solve-thermal decomposition route. Fig. S4 showed the XPS spectra survey of Al-BA-CDs. The binding energies of around 75 eV, 193 eV, 285 eV, 401 eV, 532 eV corresponded to the characteristic peaks of Al, B, C, N, and O elements. Especially, the carbonyl group (289 ev and 531.5 ev) from high temperature thermal decomposition reaction was inferred from the carboxyl of carbon dots surface. And the XPS peaks at 287 nm and 533 nm also demonstrated the presence of hydroxyl groups, which bonded to carbon atoms. We concluded that the hydroxyl group was from the carbon dots in the process of o-phenylenediamine carbonizing process, moreover, the hydroxyl group provided active binding site with aluminium ion and boric acid, which was very beneficial to fabricate Al-BA-CDs compound system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uv–vis titration spectrum of Al-BA-CDs with F
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the effective interaction between Al-BA-CDs and F-, the Uv–vis titration spectrum was obtained (Fig. 2 . a). With the addition of fluoride ion, the characteristic Uv–vis absorption peaks changed obviously. The absorption at 455 nm was classified as n-π* transition of carbonyl group and created a 415 nm red shift and the red shift corresponded to the change in color from earthy to yellow (Fig. 2b). In addition, the absorption peak at 275 nm disappeared, which moved to 250 nm. We concluded that the remarkable peak position variation was from the coordination dissociation process of [AlF6]3-, which lead to electron change of benzene ring conjugated system. The Uv–vis spectrum demonstrated that there was a strong interaction between Al-BA-CDs and F-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence selective spectrum of Al-BA-CDs for various ions
ce:section.ce:para: First, we found that the maximum emission wavelength position exhibited no obvious change with different excitation wavelengths, which demonstrated that the sensor Al-BA-CDs was no excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. S5). In addition, the fluorescence property of Al-BA-CDs depending on different pH was investigated (Fig. S6). Under strong alkaline condition, the fluorescence declined rapidly, which demonstrated that the neutral pH was appropriate testing condition. By most appropriate excitation (365 nm), the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm enhanced continuingly according to the addition of F- and reached saturation point. The performance explained again the excellent sensing property of Al-BA-CDs for F-. To evaluate the fluorescence selectivity activity, the fluorescence spectra histogram of Al-BA-CDs for various ions was studied systemically (Fig. 2c). Considering the common anions such as HCO3 –, Br-, H2PO4 -, OH–, F-, Cl-, Cr2O7 2-, PO4 3-, NO3 –, NO2 –, CO3 2– and SO4 2-, only F- resulted in obvious fluorescence occurrence, other ions could not induce fluorescence change of Al-BA-CDs water solution. Moreover, under UV light, only the Al-BA-CDs water solution after addition of F- exhibited yellow fluorescence, other water solution treated with various ions had no fluorescence (Fig. 2d). The fluorescence behavior confirmed Al-BA-CDs could be utilized for assaying F- by highly selective “off–on” fluorescence method (sensor).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence titration, competition experiment and response time of Al-BA-CDs for F
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the ion disturbance properties and detecting level of sensor Al-BA-CDs for F-, fluorescence competition (Fig. S7) and titration experiment (Fig. 2e and f) could provide important information. Firstly, other various anions were added to Al-BA-CDs water solution, the fluorescence intensity was recorded, then F- was added to above solution, the fluorescence intensity was confirmed again. Moreover, As shown in Fig. S8, the binding constant from titration experiment for F- was estimated to be 2.17 × 1010 M−1 in water media (pH = 7.4) by fitting the data to Benesi-Hildebrand equation with a good linear relationship. Additionally, the detection limit (Fig. S9) of sensor Al-BA-CDs toward F- was also confirmed by fluorescence spectrum (qmin = 3sb / S), and the detection limit could reach the 5.0 nanomolar level in pure water environment. Furthermore, according to compare the fluorescence intensities, the competition properties between F- and other anions were estimated. As shown in Fig. S7, other anions could not generate effective competition with F-. In the presence of other anions, F- could always cause the intense fluorescence of sensor Al-BA-CDs. By fluorescence competition analysis, the sensor showed strong anti-interference ability. Moreover, the reponse time of the “off–on” sensor to F- in water solution was confirmed according to the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S10). With 2 s, the fluorescence intensity could reach a peak, which demonstrated that Al-BA-CDs showed a short response time. The response time was beneficial to detecting F-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The zebrafish imaging and cell imaging of Al-BA-CDs for F
ce:section.ce:para: To study the fluorescence imaging property in living organism, the zebrafish imaging was obtained (Part S1, Fig. 3 ). The zebrafish eggs and zebrafish treated with Al-BA-CDs water solution showed no fluorescence signal, when F- water solution injected into zebrafish eggs and baby, intense yellow fluorescence signal appeared. And we could observe the zebrafish outline very well by the bright fluorescent signal. Moreover, we found that Al-BA-CDs penetrated into the inside a zebrafish, indicating that the sensor Al-BA-CDs showed good biocompatibility in zebrafish organism.

Furthermore, in order to evaluate the sensing property of sensor Al-BA-CDs for F- in organism, the cell imaging in the sensing process was investigated systemically (Part S2). As shown in Fig. 4 , the cell dandled with Al-BA-CDs water solution showed no fluorescence signal, after adding F-, the cell was lit up and yellow fluorescence signal appeared. The cell morphology was clearly shown, which indicated that Al-BA-CDs could be used as a highly selective sensor for detecting F- in living organism. Our in vivo study indicates that Al-BA-CDs possessed both high biocompatibility and selective fluorescence imaging in zebrafish and cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The simple paper chromatogram of Al-BA-CDs for detecting F
ce:section.ce:para: Simultaneously, in order to better use the fluorescent probe for detecting F-, we made a simple paper chromatogram (Fig. S11. a and Fig. S11. b). The paper was soaked with Al-BA-CDs water solution and dried naturally. Under UV light, the paper showed no fluorescence. When 1 μM F- water solution dripped onto the filter paper, Strong fluorescence appears from the filter paper. The paper chromatogram provided us with a very easy method for detecting F- in environment. According to spectra analysis and fluorescence imaging analysis, the nano system Al-BA-CDs was a very interesting fluorescence sensor for F-. In addition, the recognition mechanism detecting F- needed be preliminarily confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The inferred mechanism of sensor Al-BA-CDs for sensing F
ce:section.ce:para: The strong coordination ability between Al3+ and F - was the key factor to the “off–on” fluorescence probe Al-BA-CDs. As F - gradually enter into sensor system, they reacted with Al3+ ions in the probe system and formed stable [AlF6]3- complex. With the consumption of Al3+ from Al-BA-CDs, the fluorescence of the probe could be recovered. To deduce the detailed identification process, some crystalline substance precipitated slowly from the water solution of Al-BA-CDs + F-, which showed intense yellow fluorescence (Fig. S12). By SEM (Fig. S13) images, the crystalline substance showed markedly different morphology, and we observed amounts of carbon dots (lattice distance = 0.21 nm) in the system according to HRTEM (Fig. S14). Interestingly, when Al3+ were gradually added to the water solution from above crystalline system (BA-CDs), the strong fluorescence was quenched again (Fig. S15). Through careful analysis, aluminum ions play an important role in the fluorescence sensing process of F - . To demonstrate fluorescence differences between Al-BA-CDs and Al-BA-CDs + F-, the absolute quantum yield in water environment were measured by steady state-transient fluorescence. Before addition of F- (pH = 9.0), the quantum yield of Al-BA-CDs was only 3.85 %. After addition of F-, the quantum yield of natural crystalline substance from Al-BA-CDs + F- water solution achieved 34.68 %. The fluorescence quantum yield differences were consistent with macroscopical fluorescence properties of Al-BA-CDs and Al-BA-CDs + F- water solution.

To monitor some compounds in the process of fluorescence sensing, the HRMS (Fig. S16) and 1HNMR (Fig. S17) of Al-BA-CDs and Al-BA-CDs + F - was tested systemically. Compared with HRMS of Al-BA-CDs and Al-BA-CDs + F-, they showed significant difference. Especially, in the process of sensing F-, the structure of Al-BA-CDs changed radically due the combination of F- and Al3+. According to analysis HRMS, we deduced some existing species in sensor Al-BA-CDS system, which was helpful to understand the sensing process and mechanism. Simultaneously, according to 1HNMR titration spectra of Al-BA-CDs with F-, the 1HNMR peaks at 5.0 ppm produced a significant shift to 4.62 ppm, which as from boric acid. We deduced that the obvious chemical shift was generated from the interaction change between aluminium ion and boric acid in F-sensing process, which proved that the 1HNMR titration results could support the deduced sensing mechanism (Fig. S12).

According to one-pot hydrothermal reaction, a highly selective F- fluorescence sensor Al-BA-CDs by “off–on” process was prepared. In Al-BA-CDs, boric acid containing rich hydroxyl groups could anchored carbon dots and aluminium ion, which formed a water soluble complex combination system. In the system, aluminium ion effectively quenched the fluorescence of carbon dots in pure water environment. After addition of F-, based on stronger binding capacity with aluminium ion, aluminum ion was released from Al-BA-CDs, then the intense fluorescence from carbon dots appeared. And it showed very sensitive detecting property. To enhance the application of sensor in organism, the HepG2 cell and zebra fish fluorescence imaging were obtained. We found that the sensor Al-BA-CDs was very easy to detect F- by fluorescence “off–on” process. In addition, by paper chromatography, the sensor Al-BA-CDs exhibited highly selective sensing property for F-, which was a simple method for analyzing F- in environment. Moreover, we deduced that the sensing mechanism was from “off–on” process of fluorescence. All these results indicated that Al-BA-CDs could be utilized as a promising F- fluorescence sensor in pure water environment.

Zengchen Liu: Conceptualization. Weijie Yang: Data curation. Wenping Zhu: Data curation. Hongyu Chen: Data curation. Yahong Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Ruilan Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Yali Cui: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Yanxia Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Baodui Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Science Foundation of China (21701203) and the Henan Provincial Foundation for the Scientific and Technological Program (Grant No. 17A150057, 222102320047). Postgraduate Education Reform and Quality Improvement Project of Henan Province (YJS2023JD37). Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300420396, 242300421636).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110996.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prevention and reversal of dental caries: Role of low level fluoride

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. D. B. Featherstone, Prevention and reversal of dental caries: Role of low level fluoride, Community Dent. Oral Epidemiol. 27 (1999) 31–40.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective, fast-response, and regenerable metal-organic framework for sampling excess fluoride levels in drinking water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.M. Ebrahim, T.N. Nguyen, S. Shyshkanov, A. Gladysiak, P. Favre, A. Zacharia, G. Itskos, P.J. Dyson, K.C. Stylianou, Selective, Fast-Response, and Regenerable Metal-Organic Framework for Sampling Excess Fluoride Levels in Drinking Water, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 141 (2019) 3052–3058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Benzoxazole-linked ultrastable covalent organic frameworks for photocatalysis
